source_metadata.title_raw: Michael J. Ciccozzi - A-19-022 - May 9,2019 - Placerville

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 9581 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Michael J. Ciccozzi 
County Counsel 
El Dorado County 
330 Fair Lane 
Placerville, CA 95667 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-022 
Dear Mr. Ciccozzi: 
May 9, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding Government Code Section 1090, et 
seq. 1 Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or 
common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re 
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and 
accurate. 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the El Dorado County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1 ( c)(5).) 
QUESTION 
Does Section 1090 prohibit NEO Connect from contracting with the County as a designer 
and/or project manager in the implementation of the broadband project because NEO Connect 
performed the Broadband Feasibility Study? 
CONCLUSION 
No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the County from entering into a design and/or project 
manager contract with NEO Connect because, based on the facts provided, NEO Connect did not 
participate in the making of the design and/or project manager.contract through its performance of 
Broadband Feasibility Study, and NEO Connect has not imposed considerable or undue influence 
over the County regarding the design and/or project manager contract by the work it performed 
under the Broadband Feasibility Study. 
1 Government Code Sections 1090 through 1097.5. All statutory references are to the Government Code, 
unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 
through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of 
the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR 
File No. A-19-022 
Page No. 2 
Similar to many other rural counties, the County of El Dorado ("County") has been unable 
to attract commercial development of broadband services to serve the more rural regions of the 
County. The County Board of Supervisors ("Board") has heard from numerous residents and 
business owners that the lack of adequate internet services has a significant and detrimental 
impact on our community. In response t_o these concerns, the County issued a request for 
proposals ("RFP") for experts in the broadband field who could provide oversight, assessment, 
and advice on developing broadband services throughout the County. The County received few 
qualified responses to the RFP due to the lack of interested and experienced applicants in this 
region. Fortunately, in 2017 the County was able to contract with NEO Connect, a privately held 
company from Colorado with extensive experience in providing the necessary expertise and 
services. 
NEO Connect presented its findings and recommendations in September of 2018 and the 
Board identified specific areas that warranted further investigation. The original contract was 
amended to accomplish these tasks, and one additional agreement is pending for NEO Connect to 
complete the feasibility study. 
In anticipation of the completion of the additional tasks as part of the Feasibility Study, an 
evaluation of the legal issues related to Section 1090 was initiated to determine whether the County 
could further contract with NEO Connect. 
Project Background 
For several years, the County has been eagerly exploring options for providing adequate 
broadband services to its largely rural county. In 2017, the County was able to obtain approximately 
$75,000.00 in a matching grant from the U.S. Economic Development Administration ("EDA") to 
formally pursue a broadband feasibility study. 
In January of 2017, the County released an RFP to explore the provision and quality of 
broadband services throughout the County of EI Dorado. Tasks were identified in the RFP as: 1) 
provisions of project management services, 2) refinement of project goals with staff and Technical 
Advisory Group (TAG), 3) development of region/area profiles, 4) updating of California Public 
Utility Commission coverage map, 5) identification of key issues for broadband expansion, 6) 
outlining and prioritizing region/area strategies, 7) preparing a comprehensive presentation on 
organization and network options, and 8) identification of a "road map" for each commµnity 
region/areas, identifying "next steps." 
On September 20, 2017, the County contracted with NEO Connect, identifying the specific 
tasks from the RFP as described above. 
On September 18, 2018, NEO Connect's CEO, Diane Kruse, presented their findings and 
recommendations to the Board. Ms. Kruse identified some potential paths forward for the County, 
including models based on further encouragement of private broadband companies to expand the 
market, the exploration of a wholesale public-private partnership, and consideration of the County 
entering the market as the retail internet service provider. Ms. Kruse discussed the financial 


File No. A-19-022 
Page No. 3 
feasibility of the different models, including the finding that neither the wholesale or retail models 
were financially feasible unless outside funding was obtained ( e.g., grant funding or new tax 
revenue). NEO Connect's recommendations for "next steps" included: 
• 
Exploring broadband-friendly ordinances/policies (such as a tax revenue ordinance or a 
"shadow conduit" requirement)2, 
• 
Further refining capital costs and financial assumptions, 
• 
Exploring public-private partnerships, 
• 
Seeking outside grant funding, 
• 
Conducting "ride-outs" to assess existing utility poles and test geographic assumptions, 
• 
Engaging with the community to assess level of commitment/interest in broadband capital 
improvements, and, 
• 
Considering the development of pilot projects. 
At the end of the NEO Connect presentation, several Board members expressed interest in 
pursuing the recommended "next steps," particularly the exploration of a pilot project that would 
target both rural and populated areas to assess the feasibility of a County-wide project. Ms. Kruse 
agreed that those would be favorable areas to target. The Assistant Chief Administrative Officer of 
the County also highlighted the need to conduct further assessment of the financial assumptions that 
were used in the present study. The Board directed the CAO's Office to explore additional funding 
resources and assess what can be accomplished with available funds. 
In October and November of 2018, the CA O's Office explored funding issues. Of the 
original $149,993.00 of total funding (NEA grant money matched by County funds), $23,993.00 
had not been utilized under the original NEO Connect contract. Therefore, the County and NEO 
Connect agreed to amend the original contract to accomplish some of the "next step" tasks 
identified at the September 18, 2018 Board meeting. On December 19, 2018, the contract was 
amended to provide for additional evaluation of financial assumptions, consideration of various 
levels of property tax assessments, and review of geographical and construction cost assumptions. 
In December of2018 the CAO's Office also identified an additional $42,000 of County 
funding that could be utilized for NEO Connect to address a portion of the expanded range of "next 
steps" favored by the Board. The County is in the process of contracting with NEO Connect to 
complete the feasibility study goals by assessing broadband-friendly ordinances/policies, refining 
capital costs and financial assumptions, evaluating public-private partnerships, assisting the County 
in seeking outside grant funding, conducting "ride-outs" to assess existing utility poles and test 
geographic assumptions, assisting the County in engagement with the community to assess level of 
commitment/interest in broadband capital improvements, and, evaluating the development of 
potential pilot projects. 
The County is preparing for the next phase of contracting, aimed at the design and 
construction of the broadband fiber system. Based on the limited responses to the original RFP, and 
the apparent lack of interested regional providers who possess expertise comparable to NEO 
2 A "Shadow Conduit Policy" could require the installation of additional conduit in the public right of way 
when a permit is processed on behalf of telecommunications providers, to reduce additional costs to the County in the 
future installation of broadband fiber. 


File No. A-19-022 
Page No. 4 
Connect, the County is concerned about finding a qualified contractor to oversee the future 
implementation of the broadband project (i.e. the design/build phase of the project). You have also 
noted that the Feasibility Study will be included with the RFP for the design/build phase of the 
project, and that NEO Confect's findings have been made public in a white paper presented to the 
County. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at 
actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. 
Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
The term "public official" is interpreted broadly under Section 1090 and includes 
"independent contractors who perform a public function" and "whose official capacities carry the 
potential to exert considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency." (See 
Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 1114, 1124-1125; 
citing California Housing Finance Agency v. Hanover/California Management & Accounting 
Center, Inc. (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 682, 690-693; see also Davis v. Fresno Unified School District 
(2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, at pp. 300-301.) It'is also clear that Section 1090 applies to 
independent contractors as well as corporate consultants. (Davis supra, at p. 300.) 
Courts have long found that independent contractors that serve in advisory positions that 
have a potential to exert considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency 
are subject to Section 1090. (See Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (2010) 186 
Cal.App.4th 1114, 1124-1125; Schaefer v. Berinstein (1956) 140 Cal.App.2d 278,291 ["statutes 
prohibiting personal interests of public officers in public contracts are strictly enforced. [Citation.] 
... [,] A person merely in an advisory position to a city is affected by the conflicts of interest rule"].) 
This long-standing rule was recently affirmed by the California Supreme Court (People v. Superior 
Court (Sahlolbei) (2017) 3 Cal.5th 230), and it applies equally to corporate consultants. (Davis v. 
Fresno Unified School District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, 300.) 
The purpose behind this inclusiveness of the definition is to ensure that independent 
contractors who are essentially performing a public function, though temporarily, provide the same 
"fealty expected from permanent officers and employees." (46 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen 74 (1965).) In 
.Hub City, the court stated that a person's status as an official under Section 1090 "turns on the 
extent _to which the person influences an agency's contracting decisions or otherwise acts in a 
capacity that demands the public trust." (Hub City, supra, at p. 1125.) 
Common considerations articulated in case law as to whether a contractor or consultant 
would be subject to Section 1090 have included whether the contractor: has the potential to exert 
considerable influence; is acting in a capacity that demands a level of public trust; and has a level of 
involvement in decisions that is more than ministerial and goes beyond mere technical input. 


File No. A-19-022 
Page No. 5 
Here, the County contracted with NEO Connect in 2017 to develop a feasibility study, 
which explored three different approaches to implementing broadband within the county (a 
wholesale model, public-private partnership, and a retail model) as well as recommendations to 
implement broadband friendly ordinances, estimates on capital costs, and funding options. Under 
these facts, NEO Connect provided services that went beyond technical input and acted in a 
capacity that demands the public trust. 
However, the determinative question is whether NEO Connect's work on the initial contract 
resulted in improper influence over the subsequent contract for the implementation of the 
broadband project under a future RFP. We do not believe it does. 
In Davis, supra, a school district owned land and leased it to a contractor that would build 
school facilities on the site, and lease the improvements and site back to the district. (Davis, supra, 
at p. 271.) The contracts were a site lease and a facilities lease (collectively, the Lease-Leaseback 
Contracts). (Ibid.) But prior to awarding the Lease-Leaseback Contracts, it was alleged the district 
had entered into a different agreement with the contractor to serve as a consultant to develop plans, 
specifications and other construction documents for the new school facilities. (Id. at p. 295.) The 
court of appeal found that, for purposes of demurrer, the allegations were sufficient to show the 
contractor participated in making of the Lease-Leaseback Contracts. (Id. at p. 301.) 
In the Chadwick Advice Letter, No A-15-147, an independent contractor was involved in 
designing a golf course project that it then wished to bid on and build. There, the contractor entered 
into a contract with the city to develop a general plan that would lay out the design of a 
reconstructed golf course. The contractor advised the city, worked closely with city staff and the 
project manager, and ultimately designed and developed the plan that later became the RFP. The 
contractor had been intricately involved in designing the RFP, which was specific on exactly how 
the project was to be completed. We concluded that Section 1090 prohibited the independent 
contractor from bidding on the city contract. 
The present situation is distinguishable from those discussed above. In all of them, the 
independent contractors had the opportunity to participate in their official capacities in the making 
of subsequent contracts in which they had a financial interest. Moreover, their extensive 
involvement in previous phases of the projects provided improper influence in obtaining subsequent 
contracts for the projects. 
Here, there is no suggestion that the feasibility study conducted by NEO Connect resulted in 
extensive involvement in the subsequently proposed project and contract or improper influence over 
the development of a subsequent contract for design and/or project management. Accordingly, 
Section 1090 does not prohibit the County from entering a contract with NEO Connect for design 
and/or project management. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 


ZW:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Zachary W. Norton 
FileNo. A-19-022 
Page No. 6 
tr 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

sections.analysis:
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their

Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.)

citing California Housing Finance Agency v. Hanover/California Management & Accounting 
Center, Inc. (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 682, 690-693; see also Davis v. Fresno Unified School District 
(2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, at pp. 300-301.) It'is also clear that Section 1090 applies to

had not been utilized under the original NEO Connect contract. Therefore, the County and NEO 
Connect agreed to amend the original contract to accomplish some of the "next step" tasks 
identified at the September 18, 2018 Board meeting. On December 19, 2018, the contract was 
amended to provide for additional evaluation of financial assumptions, consideration of various 
levels of property tax assessments, and review of geographical and construction cost assumptions. 
In December of2018 the CAO's Office also identified an additional $42,000 of County 
funding that could be utilized for NEO Connect to address a portion of the expanded range of "next 
steps" favored by the Board. The County is in the process of contracting with NEO Connect to 
complete the feasibility study goals by assessing broadband-friendly ordinances/policies, refining 
capital costs and financial assumptions, evaluating public-private partnerships, assisting the County 
in seeking outside grant funding, conducting "ride-outs" to assess existing utility poles and test 
geographic assumptions, assisting the County in engagement with the community to assess level of 
commitment/interest in broadband capital improvements, and, evaluating the development of 
potential pilot projects. 
The County is preparing for the next phase of contracting, aimed at the design and 
construction of the broadband fiber system. Based on the limited responses to the original RFP, and 
the apparent lack of interested regional providers who possess expertise comparable to NEO 
2 A "Shadow Conduit Policy" could require the installation of additional conduit in the public right of way 
when a permit is processed on behalf of telecommunications providers, to reduce additional costs to the County in the 
future installation of broadband fiber.  
Connect, the County is concerned about finding a qualified contractor to oversee the future 
implementation of the broadband project (i.e. the design/build phase of the project). You have also 
noted that the Feasibility Study will be included with the RFP for the design/build phase of the 
project, and that NEO Confect's findings have been made public in a white paper presented to the 
County.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 9581 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Michael J. Ciccozzi County Counsel El Dorado County 330 Fair Lane Placerville, CA 95667 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-19-022 Dear Mr. Ciccozzi: May 9, 2019 This letter responds to your request for advice regarding Government Code Section 1090, et seq. 1 Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the El Dorado County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1 ( c)(5).) QUESTION Does Section 1090 prohibit NEO Connect from contracting with the County as a designer and/or project manager in the implementation of the broadband project because NEO Connect performed the Broadband Feasibility Study? CONCLUSION No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the County from entering into a design and/or project manager contract with NEO Connect because, based on the facts provided, NEO Connect did not participate in the making of the design and/or project manager.contract through its performance of Broadband Feasibility Study, and NEO Connect has not imposed considerable or undue influence over the County regarding the design and/or project manager contract by the work it performed under the Broadband Feasibility Study. 1 Government Code Sections 1090 through 1097.5. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR File No. A-19-022 Page No. 2 Similar to many other rural counties, the County of El Dorado ("County") has been unable to attract commercial development of broadband services to serve the more rural regions of the County. The County Board of Supervisors ("Board") has heard from numerous residents and business owners that the lack of adequate internet services has a significant and detrimental impact on our community. In response t_o these concerns, the County issued a request for proposals ("RFP") for experts in the broadband field who could provide oversight, assessment, and advice on developing broadband services throughout the County.
